:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

New Court Commits vs. Parole Revocations

While the public often envisions a prison population primarily made up of individuals serving long sentences for serious crimes, the reality is far more nuanced. In particular, the distinction between new court commits—those incarcerated for a new crime—and parole revocations—those returned to prison for violating the conditions of their parole—is a critical one. Understanding the proportion of these two groups within the prison population can provide valuable insights into the effectiveness of the state's criminal justice policies, including sentencing and parole practices.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Population Trends


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Each crime in Louisiana appears to have a statutory maximum. For example, theft of something with a value of $25,000 or over will result in imprisonment of not more than twenty years or a fine of not more than fifty thousand dollars, or both. Any sentence to imprisonment must be “determinate,” in other words, judges sentence individuals to a maximum sentence rather than a range with minimum and maximum limits. Eligibility for parole is determined by type of crime and by criminal history. Unusually, prisoners who have been convicted of non-violent, non-sexual offenses can also age out of the Louisiana prison system even if they are ineligible for parole. If sentenced to a term of over 30 years, a prisoner who has served at least 20 of those years and has reached the age of 45 is eligible for parole consideration. If sentenced to any term without the benefit of parole, a prisoner who has reached the age of 60 and has completed various institutional requirements may be eligible for parole.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 2,743 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 10% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, Unknown people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 100% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 95% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 25-34 years old made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 28% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Something like “After controlling for differences in ages, offenses, and sentence length, 66% of White people, 40% of Black people, and 55% of Hispanic people are released before/on parole eligibility date.
